name: Augeas
description: Augeas was a king of Elis in Greek mythology, son of Helios, the sun god, and Nausidame. He was known for his immense wealth, particularly his vast herds of cattle. These herds were legendary, and their stables had not been cleaned for decades, accumulating an unbelievable amount of dung. Augeas's most famous association is with the fifth labor of Heracles (Hercules). Eurystheus, the king of Tiryns, tasked Heracles with cleaning the Augean stables in a single day, an seemingly impossible task designed to humiliate the hero. Heracles, however, cleverly diverted the rivers Alpheus and Peneus to flow through the stables, washing away the accumulated filth. Augeas had promised Heracles a tenth of his cattle as payment, but after the task was completed, he refused to honor his agreement. This led to a conflict between Heracles and Augeas, which ultimately resulted in Heracles killing Augeas and installing his son Phyleus as king. The story of Augeas and his stables serves as a symbol of immense filth and corruption, and Heracles's labor represents the triumph over such overwhelming uncleanness. The cleansing of the Augean stables has become a proverbial expression for clearing away a vast amount of accumulated corruption or filth. Augeas’s greed and broken promise highlight the importance of integrity and the consequences of dishonesty. His story is a cautionary tale about the dangers of wealth and the importance of keeping one's word.
subtitle: King of Elis, owner of filthy stables.

keyMyths.title: The Fifth Labor: Cleaning the Stables
keyMyths.description: Heracles was tasked with cleaning Augeas's stables in a single day. He accomplished this seemingly impossible feat by diverting two rivers through the stables.

keyMyths.title: Augeas's Broken Promise
keyMyths.description: Augeas refused to pay Heracles the agreed-upon tenth of his cattle after the stables were cleaned. This betrayal led to a future conflict.

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Revenge
keyMyths.description: Years later, Heracles returned to Elis, killed Augeas, and installed his son Phyleus as king. This act avenged Augeas's broken promise.

sources.author: Apollodorus
